student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Interview Simulation -- Positioning Yourself as a Railway Signaling Expert for International Roles
lesson.menu_desc: It's March again, and the interview you've been waiting for is 45 minutes away -- a European railway signaling company, a real chance to pursue an international role. Tonight you stop hoping the words will come and you build them. First the register of self-positioning -- domain expertise, hands-on experience, technical liaison, to take ownership of, to make a compelling case, to add measurable value -- the language that makes you sound senior rather than rehearsed. Then the three structures that turn a fact into authority, all of which you already own: inversion ("Not only did I manage the technical scope, but I also coordinated three international suppliers"), cleft sentences ("It was the São Francisco commissioning that gave me my first exposure to international standards"), and the mixed conditional with modal perfects ("Had I not worked on CBTC commissioning, I wouldn't have the hands-on experience I have today"). You read a real person specification the way a hiring manager reads your CV, you sit the full mock interview with Claire Dubois of a European signaling firm, and you deliver your own "Tell me about yourself" -- your real experience at Motiva, São Francisco, CBTC, the post-graduation in project management -- as a case, not a hope. This is an integration lesson: no new grammar, every structure you have, put to work under interview pressure -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 19 -- Interview Simulation
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 19 -- Interview Simulation

listenings.text: Tell me about yourself. Fifteen years in railway signaling, and I still start the same way, because a hiring manager is not asking for your life story, they are asking for your case. So I lead with what I am, not where I was born. I'm a signaling engineer with deep domain expertise in CBTC commissioning, and hands-on experience taking systems from the factory acceptance test all the way to live running on metro lines. That's the headline. Then I give it a spine, one project that proves it. It was the commissioning of a driverless line that gave me my first real exposure to international standards, and on that project I acted as the technical liaison between the operator and three overseas suppliers at once. Notice I don't just list tasks. I took ownership of the interface management, I demonstrated initiative when I proposed a risk register no one had asked for, and by the end that register had caught two faults before they reached the track. That's measurable value, and a hiring manager remembers the number, not the adjective. Then I close with why I'm in the room. Had I not spent those years on live commissioning, I wouldn't have the judgment I bring now, and that judgment is exactly what an international role rewards. I'm not looking to leave a job, I'm looking to add measurable value on a bigger stage, and to contribute to the broader goals of a company that works across borders. Keep it to ninety seconds. Make a compelling case, not a confession. And whatever you do, address the hard questions head-on, because the candidate who owns the difficult part is the one they trust with the easy part.

Three things before you walk into that interview, from someone who has coached a lot of engineers through exactly this door. First, they are not testing your English, they are testing whether you can make them confident. So stop translating your CV and start making a case. Every answer should quietly say I have done this, here is the proof, and here is what it will do for you. That is the difference between sounding rehearsed and sounding senior. Second, use the structures that carry authority, and you already have them, so use them on purpose. When you say not only did I manage the scope, but I also ran the supplier interface, the inversion makes the sentence lean forward, it sounds like leadership. When you say it was the São Francisco project that taught me international standards, the cleft puts the spotlight exactly where you want it. And when you say had I not worked on live commissioning, I wouldn't have this judgment, the conditional turns your past into the reason to hire you. These are not tricks, they are emphasis, and emphasis is what a nervous candidate forgets. Third, the hardest question is a gift. When they ask about a conflict with a supplier, or your greatest professional challenge, do not deflect and do not shrink. Address it head-on, take ownership of your part, and show what you changed because of it. The engineer who says here is what went wrong and here is what I now do differently is far stronger than the one with no scars. Lead with your expertise, prove it with one project, and let the difficult question be the moment you look most in control. That is how you turn forty-five minutes into an offer.

patterns.title: Three structures you already own -- now used to sound senior
cards: Put a negative or limiting word first and the sentence leans forward like leadership. "Not only did I manage the technical scope, but I also coordinated three international suppliers." "Never before had the team commissioned a driverless line." After the fronted word the subject and verb swap places -- did I, had the team -- exactly as in a question. It is emphasis, not decoration: it makes the achievement land before the detail.

Split one sentence into two halves to highlight the part that matters. "It was the São Francisco commissioning that gave me my first exposure to international standards" -- the cleft frames one project as THE turning point. "What I brought to that team was interface management." Use it in an interview to decide, yourself, which fact the manager remembers -- instead of leaving it buried in a list.
Turn your past into the reason to hire you

"Had I not worked on CBTC commissioning, I wouldn't have the hands-on experience I have today" -- a past cause with a present result. "If I had been given the lead from day one, I would have implemented a risk register immediately" -- a past regret with a past result (modal perfect: would have + participle). The conditional lets you present experience as cause and value as effect, which is exactly what a hiring manager is listening for.

patterns.text: None of these three is new -- you have met inversion, cleft sentences and the conditionals across this course. What changes tonight is the PURPOSE: in an interview they are not grammar, they are emphasis. Inversion makes an achievement lean forward; a cleft decides which fact the manager remembers; a mixed conditional turns your past into cause and your value into effect. A nervous candidate flattens everything into a list -- "I did this, I did that." A senior one chooses where the spotlight falls. Same experience, opposite impression.

items.situation: "Tell me about yourself." You have ninety seconds. Lead with what you are and your domain expertise -- not where you were born.
tips: I'm a signaling engineer with deep domain expertise in CBTC commissioning, and hands-on experience taking systems from factory acceptance tests all the way to live running.
My field is railway signaling -- specifically driverless-line commissioning -- and what I bring is fifteen years of hands-on experience, not just the theory.

items.situation: "Walk me through your experience with CBTC systems." Use a cleft to frame one project as the turning point.
tips: It was the São Francisco commissioning that gave me my first real exposure to CBTC and international standards -- I took it from factory tests to live running.
What that project taught me was how a CBTC system behaves under real traffic; it was there that I learned to read the standard, not just apply it.

items.situation: "Describe a conflict you had with a supplier and how you resolved it." Address it head-on, take ownership of your part.
tips: We disputed a delay claim; I took ownership of the interface records, showed the dated evidence, and we settled without prejudice to either side -- and I changed how I log approvals because of it.
There was a real conflict over a schedule overrun. I addressed it head-on, owned the gap in our own documentation, and put in a tracker so it couldn't happen again.

items.situation: "Why are you looking for an international position?" Make a compelling case -- add measurable value, not escape.
tips: I'm not looking to leave a job; I'm pursuing an international role where I can add measurable value on a bigger stage and contribute to the broader goals of a cross-border programme.
I want to work where signaling is delivered across borders -- that's where my domain expertise adds the most measurable value, and where I keep growing.

items.situation: "What's your greatest professional challenge right now?" Use a mixed conditional to turn it into strength.
tips: Communicating my judgment in English at full pace -- but had I not pushed myself into international projects, I wouldn't be addressing it as directly as I am now.
Leading suppliers I don't manage directly. If I hadn't taken ownership of that interface early, I wouldn't have learned to influence without authority the way I do today.

items.situation: "Where do you see yourself in five years?" Show initiative and best practices, not just a title.
tips: Leading commissioning on international programmes, implementing best practices across teams, and being the technical liaison a client trusts with the hardest interface.
Running signaling delivery on a cross-border line -- demonstrating the kind of initiative that turns a good project into a benchmark others copy.

bank.label: Leading with expertise (Tell me about yourself)
items: I'm a signaling engineer with deep domain expertise in CBTC commissioning and hands-on experience on live metro lines.
What I bring is fifteen years of hands-on delivery -- from factory acceptance tests to live running, not just the theory.
I acted as the technical liaison between the operator and three overseas suppliers, and I took ownership of the interface management.

bank.label: Structures that carry authority
items: Not only did I manage the technical scope, but I also coordinated three international suppliers simultaneously.
It was the São Francisco commissioning that gave me my first real exposure to international standards.
Had I not worked on CBTC commissioning, I wouldn't have the hands-on experience I have today.

bank.label: Owning the hard question
items: Let me take that head-on: here is what went wrong, here is my part in it, and here is what I now do differently.
I took ownership of the gap in our records, settled the dispute without prejudice, and implemented a tracker so it couldn't recur.
The candidate who owns the difficult part is the one you can trust with the easy part -- so I won't deflect it.

bank.label: Why an international role (making the case)
items: I'm not looking to leave a job -- I'm looking to add measurable value on a bigger stage.
I want to contribute to the broader goals of a company that delivers signaling across borders.
It's where my domain expertise adds the most measurable value, and where I keep addressing new challenges head-on.
